referred_to_by.content: This is the **second edition** (1697) of Thomas Gataker's monumental Latin edition of Marcus Aurelius' *Meditations* (*Ta eis heauton*, "To Himself"), first published in 1652. Gataker's edition is considered **"the principal authority"** on the text of the *Meditations* according to A.S.L. Farquharson, the foremost 20th-century Marcus Aurelius scholar. The 1697 edition was expanded with annotations by **André Dacier** (the leading French classical scholar of the era) and a biography attributed to "Cornelio Stanhope" — almost certainly **George Stanhope** (1660–1728), Dean of Canterbury. It was published in London by **Edward Millington**, one of the most famous book auctioneers of the 17th century, and features a portrait engraving likely by **William Faithorne the Elder** (c. 1616–1691), England's foremost portrait engraver.
4to (quarto)
A. D'Acerius (annotator), Cornelio Stanhope (biographer)

A six-volume folio **composite atlas** (*atlas factice*) published in Amsterdam by the firm of Reinier Ottens (1698–1750) and Josua Ottens (1704–1765), c.1725–1750. The Ottens brothers were among the most prominent map publishers of 18th-century Amsterdam, heirs to the firm founded by their father Joachim Ottens (1663–1719). The *Atlas Major* is not a conventionally authored work with a fixed text: it belongs to the tradition of the made-to-order luxury atlas, assembled on commission from maps by many different cartographers (F. de Wit, G. de L'Isle, H. Jaillot, N. Visscher, J.B. Homann, G. van Keulen, P. Schenk, and many others), all engraved and hand-colored. No two copies are identical. Known configurations range from 2 to 15 volumes. The Teylers copy is six volumes.
fol.

_label: "A journal of a voyage to the South seas"

identified_by.content: "A journal of a voyage to the South seas"

referred_to_by.content: The **second edition** of *A Journal of a Voyage to the South Seas, in His Majesty's Ship, the Endeavour* (London: Charles Dilly and James Phillips, 1784), by Sydney Parkinson (c.1745–1771). Parkinson served as botanical and natural history draughtsman to Sir Joseph Banks on Cook's first Pacific voyage (1768–1771). He died at sea on 26 January 1771 after contracting dysentery at Batavia. The first edition (1773) was edited controversially by his brother Stanfield Parkinson. This 1784 second edition was edited by **John Coakley Lettsom**, contains an extensive appendix (pp. [213]–353) with abridgements of Cook's second and third voyages, and includes 27 hand-coloured copper-engraved plates plus a portrait frontispiece and maps. The book is historically significant as one of the few unofficial accounts of Cook's first voyage, and the 1773 first edition contains the **first printed naming of the kangaroo** in any published book.

_label: Encyclopédie, ou Dictionnaire raisonné des sciences, des arts et des métiers

identified_by.content: Encyclopédie, ou Dictionnaire raisonné des sciences, des arts et des métiers

referred_to_by.content: The complete first edition of the **Encyclopédie, ou Dictionnaire raisonné des sciences, des arts et des métiers**, edited by **Denis Diderot** (Langres, 1713 – Paris, 1784) and initially co-edited by **Jean le Rond d'Alembert** (Paris, 1717 – Paris, 1783), published in Paris and Amsterdam, 1751–1780. The complete first edition comprises **35 folio volumes**: 17 text volumes + 11 plates volumes + 4 supplement text volumes + 1 supplement plates volume + 2 analytical index volumes (*Table analytique*). Contains 71,818 articles and 3,129 plates, with more than 130 contributors. The work was condemned by the Parlement de Paris (1759) and placed on the Catholic Church's *Index Librorum Prohibitorum* by Pope Clement XIII (1759), forcing the completion of the text volumes in secret. Acquired by Teylers **in 1780** — four years before the museum's formal opening in 1784, making it one of the earliest documented acquisitions in the collection. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken** and central to the 2026 Teylers exhibition **"Gevaarlijke Boeken."**

referred_to_by.content: This is a multi-volume octavo work by Georges Cuvier (1769–1832) surveying the progress of the natural sciences from the year of the French Revolution (1789) to the mid-1820s. It represents the expanded book form of Cuvier's official reports on scientific progress, originally commissioned by Napoleon and later continued under the Bourbon Restoration monarchs Louis XVIII and Charles X. Published by Baudouin Frères in Paris between 1826 and 1828, the work is a landmark in the historiography of science, written by the man who was simultaneously the most powerful scientific administrator in France and one of its greatest naturalists.
8° (octavo)

referred_to_by.content: This is a two-part quarto volume containing Job Baster's *Natuurkundige Uitspanningen, behelzende eenige waarneemingen over sommige zee-planten en zee-insecten, benevens derzelver zaadhuisjes en eyernesten* ("Natural Recreations, containing some observations on certain sea-plants and sea-insects, together with their seed-vessels and egg-nests"). Published by J. Bosch in Haarlem between 1759 and 1765, the work comprises Part I (169 pp, 16 plates) and Part II (167 pp, 13 plates), bound together as one volume. The 29 plates are hand-colored copperplate engravings depicting marine organisms — hydropolyps, corallines, mollusks, their eggs, and larvae. This is a foundational work in Dutch marine biology and one of the earliest systematic illustrated studies of invertebrate marine life from the North Sea and Oosterschelde.
4° (quarto); Part I: 169 pp, 16 plates; Part II: 167 pp, 13 plates; two parts bound as one

referred_to_by.content: This is the official expedition report by Otto Herz on the recovery of the **Beresovka mammoth** — one of the most famous paleontological discoveries in history. Published by the Imperial Academy of Sciences in St. Petersburg in 1902, it documents the 1901 expedition to excavate a nearly complete woolly mammoth carcass preserved in Siberian permafrost for approximately 44,000 years. The mammoth was found with undigested food still in its stomach and grass between its teeth, indicating sudden death during summer. The mounted specimen remains on display at the Zoological Museum in St. Petersburg.
gr. 8°, 38 pp, 10 plates

referred_to_by.content: This is the founding catalog of what would become **Rijksmuseum Boerhaave** — the Netherlands' national museum of science history. Written by Claude August Crommelin (1878–1965), adjunct-director of the Kamerlingh Onnes Laboratory in Leiden, it systematically documents the historical collection of 18th-century physics instruments that had been displaced by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes's modern cryogenics equipment. The catalog describes demonstration apparatus from the famous **'s Gravesande–Musschenbroek collection** — instruments used to introduce Newtonian physics to continental Europe. Published in the year of Kamerlingh Onnes's death (1926), it directly led to the founding of the Nederlandsch Historisch Natuurwetenschappelijk Museum in 1928, which Albert Einstein visited in its opening year (1931).
70 pp, 11 plates, 24 × 16 cm

referred_to_by.content: This is a bilingual Greek-and-Latin edition of two ancient philosophical texts on fate, determinism, and free will, printed by Thomas Roycroft in London in 1658. The volume is in duodecimo format with a parchment binding.

referred_to_by.content: This is the first critical edition of Ammonius's *De adfinium vocabulorum Differentia* (On the Differences of Synonymous Expressions), an ancient Greek grammatical treatise distinguishing near-synonyms. Edited by the young Lodewijk Caspar Valckenaer (1715–1785), who was only 24 at the time, and published by Jean Luzac in Leiden in 1739. The edition includes four previously unpublished ancient grammatical texts and three books of Valckenaer's own scholarly commentary. It was a landmark publication that launched Valckenaer's career as one of the greatest Dutch classical philologists.
Quarto, 6 + XXIX + 263 pp

referred_to_by.content: A red chalk drawing with white heightening on paper by **Michelangelo Buonarroti** (1475–1564), c. 1511, depicting a study for one of the **ignudi** (nude male figures) on the Sistine Chapel ceiling, most probably near the Persian Sibyl / Separation of the Earth from the Waters. Described as "probably the best-preserved example" of Michelangelo's Sistine preparatory studies. The verso (A 027 verso) contains swift sketches of figures surrounding God the Father in the **Creation of Adam** — connecting this single sheet to two of the most significant Sistine compositions. Part of approximately 22–25 Michelangelo drawings at Teylers, forming one of the **top five collections of Michelangelo drawings in the world**, acquired in 1790 via Willem Anne Lestevenon from the Odescalchi heirs in Rome for 10,000 guilders. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.

referred_to_by.content: A drawing in red chalk (with stylus underdrawing and possible graphite annotations) on paper (203 x 224 mm) by **Raphael** (Raffaello Sanzio, Urbino, 1483 – Rome, 1520), dated c. 1517–1518. The title — "Study for the half-length figures of two angels" — connects the sheet to the preparatory process for the **Transfiguration** (Pinacoteca Vaticana), Raphael's last major painting, commissioned c. 1516–1517 by Cardinal Giulio de' Medici. Part of Teylers' collection of **twelve drawings attributed to Raphael** — the largest Raphael drawings collection in the Netherlands and among the most significant in the world. Acquired in 1790 as part of the same Odescalchi purchase that brought the Michelangelo drawings to Haarlem. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.
"Rafael"; "Sassoferrato"; "A 95?"; "Teyler"
Purchased from Odescalchi heirs, Rome, 1790

This is one of the most important fossils in any museum worldwide — the so-called **"Haarlem Archaeopteryx,"** described by Teylers' own Collection Manager as **"the Mona Lisa of the museum's fossil collection."** Found in 1855 near Jachenhausen, Bavaria, it was the **chronologically first Archaeopteryx specimen ever discovered** — predating the famous London specimen by six years — but was misidentified as a pterosaur (*Pterodactylus crassipes*) by Hermann von Meyer in 1857. It remained misidentified for over a century until **John Ostrom** recognized feather impressions during a visit to Teylers in September 1970. In **2017**, it was reclassified again: Foth & Rauhut demonstrated it is not *Archaeopteryx* at all, but a new genus — **Ostromia crassipes** — the first anchiornithid dinosaur found outside China. In May 2024, the fossil was secretly transported to the Diamond Light Source synchrotron in the UK for cutting-edge X-ray analysis.

F 07424-01 is a jaw fragment of *Mosasaurus hoffmanni* (Mantell, 1829), part of the historically decisive specimen cataloged as TM 7424 at Teylers Museum. This specimen — consisting of three partial jaw fragments along with cervical vertebrae and a femur — represents the first scientifically documented remains of a mosasaur ever recovered anywhere in the world. The fossils were extracted from a chalk quarry in the Sint Pietersberg near Maastricht, Netherlands, in 1764, making them foundational to both vertebrate palaeontology and to the concept of biological extinction.

The specimen was acquired by Teylers Museum in 1784, when director Martinus van Marum purchased the palaeontological collection of Jean-Baptiste Drouin, a lieutenant colonel in the French army who had collected the remains from the Sint Pietersberg in 1766. Unlike the more famous second Maastricht skull (now MNHN AC 9648 in Paris, seized by French forces in 1795), TM 7424 was protected from confiscation because it belonged to Teylers Museum, which retained legal protections. The specimen remains on permanent display in Teylers' fossil hall.

The jaw fragment is preserved in its natural white chalk matrix, as visible in the catalog image: multiple elongated jaw bones are embedded in a cream-coloured limestone block, with the characteristic pleurodont teeth of the mosasaur clearly visible. The Maastrichtian chalk in which it is embedded is itself the type sediment for the Maastrichtian Stage of the Late Cretaceous (72–66 million years ago), making the specimen doubly significant — both the founding fossil of mosasaur science and a type locality specimen of the geological stage in which it lived.
kaak (jaw)
(none listed)

This is one of the most famous fossils in the history of science — a nearly complete skeleton of a giant salamander (*Andrias scheuchzeri*), approximately 1 meter long, discovered in 1725 near **Öhningen** on Lake Constance, Germany. In 1726, Swiss naturalist **Johann Jakob Scheuchzer** published it as *Homo diluvii testis* ("Man, witness of the Flood") — the skeleton of a human who drowned in the biblical Deluge. The fossil remained misidentified for 85 years until **Georges Cuvier** visited Teylers Museum in **1811**, carefully prepared the fossil with a needle, uncovered the forelimbs, and definitively proved it was a giant salamander. This is one of the founding demonstrations of comparative anatomy and a classic case study in how scientific understanding evolves. Purchased by Martinus van Marum in **1802** for 14 Louis d'or, the specimen remains the **lectotype** of *Andrias scheuchzeri* and is displayed in its original showcase.

F 16266 is the skull of a woolly mammoth (*Mammuthus primigenius*), discovered in 1820 near Heukelum in the Dutch province of Gelderland, and today known as "De Mammoetschedel van Heukelum." It is the largest and most complete mammoth skull found in the Netherlands, and one of the most complete of its kind in Western Europe. The animal was a male estimated at approximately 43 years of age at the time of death, and lived approximately 31,000 years ago in the Late Pleistocene (Upper Palaeolithic).

The skull was discovered on 26 January 1820 when a dike breach near Heukelum, along the Nieuwe Zuider Lingedijk, carved a 20-metre-deep hole in the land of farmer Frans van Wilgen, exposing the specimen. Museum director Martinus van Marum visited in March 1820, but van Wilgen was absent. The skull was sold at auction in 1824, purchased by the Hollandsche Maatschappij der Wetenschappen for 2,755 guilders — a sum equivalent to the cost of three houses at the time. It remained in the Hollandsche Maatschappij's Naturaliënkabinet until that collection was dissolved in 1866, after which the skull was held in storage until transfer to Teylers Museum in **1885**.

The catalog image shows the skull mounted on a wall bracket in the museum, with the two massive curved tusks displayed hanging beneath it. The skull retains both orbital regions, the frontal and parietal bones, and shows the characteristic domed cranium of *Mammuthus primigenius*. It is displayed in the First Fossil Hall.
(none listed)

F 16269 is an assembled skeletal mount of the cave bear (*Ursus spelaeus* Rosenmüller, 1794), displayed in the First Fossil Hall of Teylers Museum. As stated on the Teylers topstukken page, this is a **composite skeleton** — bones from multiple individual cave bears found together in Moravian caves were assembled to create a single articulated mount, because the provenance of individual bones to specific individuals could not be determined scientifically. The specimen comes from the Moravia region (now the Czech Republic), near Brno, and dates to approximately 20,000–24,000 years ago (Late Pleistocene, around the Last Glacial Maximum).

The catalog image shows the fully mounted skeleton on a display stand in the museum's First Fossil Hall, with what appears to be a separate large cave bear skull displayed on a pedestal to its left. The skeleton is large and robustly built, with a characteristic domed skull, prominent sagittal crest, and the large flat grinding teeth associated with the predominantly herbivorous diet now attributed to *Ursus spelaeus*. The animal would have stood approximately 1.7 metres at the shoulder and measured up to 3.5 metres when reared on its hind legs, weighing up to approximately 1,000 kg (males); significantly larger than any living bear species.

Cave bears inhabited the mountainous regions of Europe and parts of Asia during the Middle and Late Pleistocene, and became extinct approximately 24,000 years ago — before or at the onset of the Last Glacial Maximum, and well before the arrival of anatomically modern humans in most of central Europe, suggesting that climate change rather than human hunting was the primary extinction driver.
(none listed)

F 16390 is one of the infamous **Lügensteine** (German: "lying stones"; Dutch: "liegstenen") — deliberately carved limestone forgeries designed to deceive the German palaeontologist Johann Bartholomeus Adam Beringer (1667–1740), Dean of the Faculty of Medicine at the University of Würzburg. The stones were carved and planted in a hillside near Würzburg around 1725 by two colleagues who resented Beringer's arrogance: **J. Ignaz Roderick** (Professor of Geography, Algebra and Analysis at the University of Würzburg) and **Johann Georg von Eckhart** (Privy Counsellor and University Librarian). The stones depicted various organisms including insects, birds, plants, snails, worms, astronomical objects (suns, moons, comets), and Hebrew letters.

The catalog image shows a single carved pale-grey limestone slab (approximately 10–15 cm across) bearing a relief carving of what appears to be a crab or spider-like organism in the centre, with a small museum label attached in the lower right corner. The stone is irregular in outline, typical of the Lügensteine format, and the carving is in a three-dimensional raised relief style distinct from genuine fossilised impressions.

Beringer's hired diggers (three local boys from Eivelstadt) delivered him approximately 2,000 such stones between May 1725 and November 1725. He published an illustrated monograph on his "findings," titled *Lithographiae Wirceburgensis* (Würzburg: Philipp Wilhelm Fuggart, 1726), with detailed engravings of the stones. Shortly after publication he discovered the hoax, took legal action against Roderick and von Eckhart, and won his case on 13 April 1726. Both perpetrators were removed from their university positions; Roderick was banished from Würzburg. Despite this, Beringer's professional reputation never fully recovered. He died in 1740.

The Teylers Museum holds at least six Lügensteine. The stones represent one of the most celebrated cases of scientific fraud in history and are significant objects for the history of palaeontology and scientific methodology.

This is a standard measuring ruler (standaardmaatlat) made by Benjamin Ayres (active 1731–c. 1775), an English-born instrument maker established in Amsterdam. The ruler is a conversion tool comparing multiple concurrent European measurement systems — English, French, Rhineland, Amsterdam, and Holland measures — in mahogany with brass fittings. Made for or by appointment to the Prince of Orange and the Admiralty of Amsterdam, it represents the chaotic state of European metrology before the adoption of the metric system.

Op de eene zijde: de Egnelse en de Amsgterdamsche voet in 8 delen en de Fransche, Rhijnlandsche, Amsterdamsche en Hollandsche palm in 12 delen. Op de andere zijde: 2/3 yard en de Amsterdamsche el in 8 delen. Koperen maat: (div.) voeten in 8 deelen en palm in 12 d

referred_to_by.content: This is a spirit level calibration device made by Gebroeders Caminada, Rotterdam, a prominent Dutch instrument-making firm of Italian origin (active 1856–c. 1920). The device is a bench-top apparatus used to test and calibrate spirit level vials used in surveying leveling instruments. Its production period (1882–1898) coincides with the Netherlands' Eerste Nauwkeurigheidswaterpassing (First Precision Leveling Survey, 1875–1885), which demanded rigorously calibrated instruments.
"Gebr. Caminada, Rotterdam"

This is a marine box chronometer by Arnold & Dent, London, No. 782, dating from the partnership period of 1830–1840. It is a precision timekeeping instrument housed in a traditional three-tier mahogany box with brass gimbals, designed to maintain accurate time regardless of a ship's motion. The chronometer was acquired by Teylers Museum in 1870 as a replacement for an earlier chronometer that had worn out, and was used for astronomical observations at the museum's observatory. Edward John Dent, the junior partner, later became famous as the maker of the Great Clock of the Houses of Parliament (Big Ben).

This is an 18th-century physics demonstration apparatus for teaching pulley mechanics, made by the Amsterdam instrument makers **J. Kampman and J.C. Pelletier**. It consists of a mahogany stand with two brass pulleys, used to demonstrate mechanical advantage — the principle that a small force applied over a greater distance can move a larger weight. Such apparatus was standard equipment in 18th-century physics cabinets (*fysische kabinetten*) and derives from designs published by **Willem Jacob 's Gravesande** in his influential textbook *Physices Elementa Mathematica* (1720). The instrument belongs to Teylers' **Fysisch Kabinet**, one of the oldest institutional physics collections in the Netherlands, assembled from the 1780s onward by director **Martinus van Marum**.
Toestel ter demonstratie van de werking van de katrol

This is a set of 18th-century compound pulley blocks (*katrollen*) made by the Amsterdam instrument makers **J. Kampman and Jean Pelletier**, used to demonstrate mechanical advantage through block-and-tackle pulley systems. The apparatus retains its **original cord** — a rare survival of organic material on an 18th-century scientific instrument. It is one of two pulley demonstrations by Kampman & Pelletier in the Teylers collection (the other being FK 0018, a pulley stand), and forms part of a larger suite of Newtonian mechanics apparatus based on designs by **Willem Jacob 's Gravesande** (1688–1742).
Toestel ter demonstratie van de werking van de katrol

This is a set of five graduated block-and-tackle configurations mounted on a frame, demonstrating mechanical advantage from 1:1 through 5:1. Each tackle adds one more supporting rope segment and one more pulley, progressively showing the **"golden rule of mechanics"**: what is gained in force is lost in distance. The apparatus belongs to a **three-part pedagogical series** of pulley demonstrations in the Teylers Fysisch Kabinet (FK 0018 → FK 0020 → FK 0022), progressing from individual pulleys to assembled blocks to complete graduated tackle systems. All three date to 1775–1799 and derive from designs by **Willem Jacob 's Gravesande** (1688–1742).
Toestel ter demonstratie van de werking van takels [raam]

referred_to_by.content: This is an 18th-century inclined plane demonstration apparatus (*hellend vlak*), built after the design of Willem Jacob 's Gravesande (1688–1742), the pioneering Leiden professor who introduced experimental demonstration-based physics teaching to continental Europe. The instrument was made by the Amsterdam instrument-making partnership of J. Kampman and Adam Steitz, active in the third quarter of the 18th century. It belongs to the tradition of Newtonian physics demonstration apparatus that 's Gravesande codified in his landmark textbook *Physices Elementa Mathematica* (1720).
Toestel ter demonstratie van de beweging langs het hellend vlak

_label: Cycloïdebaan met verstelbare rechte helling en cycloïde slinger

identified_by.content: Cycloïdebaan met verstelbare rechte helling en cycloïde slinger

referred_to_by.content: This is an 18th-century physics demonstration instrument that embodies two of the most elegant discoveries in the history of mathematics: the **brachistochrone** (curve of fastest descent) and the **tautochrone** (curve of equal-time descent) — both of which turn out to be the same curve: the **cycloid**. The apparatus has an adjustable straight incline (to compare against a cycloidal track) and a cycloidal pendulum (demonstrating that a weight swings in equal time regardless of amplitude). It was built by **J. Kampman** in the workshop of **Adam Steitz** in Amsterdam, following the instrument designs of **Willem Jacob 's Gravesande** (1688–1742), the foremost promoter of Newtonian physics on the European continent. The instrument connects three giants of 17th-century science: **Christiaan Huygens** (who discovered the tautochrone property in 1673), **Johann Bernoulli** (who posed the brachistochrone challenge in 1696), and **Isaac Newton** (who solved it in a single evening). It is part of a coordinated suite of 's Gravesande-style mechanics apparatus at Teylers, all from the Kampman/Steitz workshop.

_label: Model van een zonnecollector met stoomzuiger

identified_by.content: Model van een zonnecollector met stoomzuiger

referred_to_by.content: A **working scale model of a parabolic solar concentrator coupled to a steam piston mechanism**, manufactured in Paris in 1879 by instrument maker A. Salleron, based on the solar engine technology developed by Augustin Mouchot (1825–1912). The device consists of a silvered parabolic dish (~48 cm diameter) on a mahogany base. A blackened copper boiler is positioned at the focal point; tilting and rotating the dish tracks the sun and concentrates radiation on the boiler. Steam from the heated water drives a piston coupled to a set of wheels, demonstrating the complete conversion of solar radiation into mechanical motion. The museum likely acquired it in 1879 in direct response to the gold-medal demonstration by Mouchot and his assistant Abel Pifre at the **1878 Exposition Universelle in Paris**. This is one of the earliest solar-to-mechanical energy conversion devices in any museum collection worldwide. A comparable model exists in the Musée des Arts et Métiers, Paris (acquired 1880).
Model van een zonnecollector met stoomzuiger

FK 0219 is a **gasometer** (*gazometer*) — a gas-measuring and gas-delivery apparatus — designed by **Martinus van Marum** (1750–1837) and built by instrument maker **Frederik Willem Fries** at Teylers Museum, Haarlem, in late 1790 to early 1791. It was specifically designed to demonstrate the combustion of hydrogen in oxygen, producing water and thereby confirming Lavoisier's proof that water is a compound (H₂O), not an element.

The device is van Marum's deliberate simplification and improvement of Lavoisier's original gasometer design (published in *Traité élémentaire de chimie*, 1789), built by Paris maker Pierre Mégnié and notoriously expensive. Van Marum's version made the water-synthesis demonstration affordable and reproducible for scientific institutions worldwide. Upon its completion van Marum received replication requests from scientific academies across Europe. Van Marum published its description in *Verhandelingen uitgegeven door Teyler's Tweede Genootschap*, Volume 10 (1798) — a primary technical source entirely absent from the catalog.

Fries was a Swiss-born instrument maker recruited by van Marum from London in 1790. He was introduced to Teylers' Second Society on 19 November 1790 and had left Haarlem by April 1791 due to homesickness — the tight production date "1790–1791" is fully confirmed by primary records.
"Verbrandingstoestel voor waterstof van Martinus van Marum"
Not recorded

A **phonautograph** (*fonautograaf*) — the **world's first practical device to record sound as a visible waveform**, predating Edison's phonograph by 20 years. Invented by French typographer **Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville** (25 April 1817 – 26 April 1879), patented in France on **25 March 1857** (French patent no. 17,897/31,470), and manufactured by Prussian-born Paris instrument maker **Karl Rudolph Koenig** (1832–1901). Koenig began manufacturing phonautographs from 1859; the Smithsonian acquired a comparable example from Koenig in 1866 for 500 francs.

**Operating principle:** Sound enters a large conical horn (white-enameled interior, brass/zinc body), causing a taut membrane at the narrow end to vibrate. A boar's bristle attached to the membrane traces a wavy line — a *phonautogram* — in lampblack soot on a rotating cylinder driven by a hand crank. The device **cannot play back sound**; it produces only a visual record of sound waves. This is the critical functional distinction from Edison's phonograph (1877).

**Physical appearance:** Large rectangular wooden base (840 × 730 mm). Dominant feature: large conical horn with white-enameled interior, supported by ornate cast-iron brackets. Hand crank drives the horizontal recording cylinder via a pulley wheel. Cast-iron A-frame support. Fine-grained hardwood base.

Teylers acquired this instrument in **1865**, the same year as its listed date.

A **heliostat** (*zonnevolger*) built on the design of German astronomer **Wilhelm Klinkerfues** (1827–1884) of the Göttingen Observatory, manufactured by Göttingen precision instrument maker **Meyerstein**, dated 1865. A heliostat is an instrument equipped with a clock-driven mirror that continuously rotates to compensate for the Earth's rotation, reflecting a steady beam of sunlight in a fixed direction — used in spectroscopy, photometry, and optical experiments requiring a stable light source.

Klinkerfues, professor of astronomy and director of the Göttingen Observatory from 1868, designed a distinctive heliostat type and published the design in *Astronomische Nachrichten*. The designation "naar Klinkerfues" (after Klinkerfues) in the title confirms this instrument implements his published design. Meyerstein was the leading precision instrument workshop in Göttingen in the mid-19th century, supplying the university's physics and astronomy departments.

A **seven-foot Newtonian reflecting telescope** built by **William Herschel** (1738–1822) at his workshop in Slough, England, and acquired by Teylers Museum director **Martinus van Marum** in 1790 after visiting Herschel's observatory in person and observing nebulae through his instruments. Van Marum purchased the instrument for the small observatory on the roof of the Teylers Oval Room.

The telescope features an **octagonal mahogany tube** of approximately 7-foot (2.1 m) focal length, a **speculum metal primary mirror** (~155 mm / 6⅛ inch aperture), a diagonal secondary mirror directing light to a perpendicular eyepiece (confirming Newtonian design), mounted on a mahogany altazimuth stand with brass fittings. Herschel made hundreds of such instruments commercially — by 1795 he claimed to have made over 200 seven-foot mirrors alone. This telescope is a smaller sibling of the instrument Herschel used to **discover Uranus on 13 March 1781**.

A second listed maker, **Frédéric Guillaume Fries**, was a Swiss-born instrument maker previously working in London whom van Marum recruited to Haarlem in 1790, introducing him to Teylers' Second Society in November of that year. His specific role in this instrument (maintenance, assembly, or adaptation) is not established.

The **Van Marum Large Electrostatic Generator** — the **largest flat-plate frictional electrostatic machine ever built**, and one of the most important surviving scientific instruments of the 18th century. It remains the centerpiece of the Teylers Museum instrument room. Two coaxial glass discs (1.65 m diameter each) rotate against friction cushions, generating static charge collected by brass combs and stored in a battery of approximately 100 Leyden jars across four complete sets — the largest Leyden jar battery ever assembled. Capable of generating approximately 330,000 V (sparks to 60 cm); combined with the full battery, voltages approaching 720 kV. **Van Marum first observed the smell of ozone using this machine in 1785**, though he did not identify the gas as such.

Commissioned by **Martinus van Marum** (1750–1837), first director of Teylers Museum, from instrument maker **John Cuthbertson** in May 1783. Cuthbertson was paid 3,250 guilders. The wooden support and cabinetwork were designed by architect **Leendert Viervant the Younger** (1752–**1801**). The machine was placed in the newly opened Oval Room on **Christmas Day (eerste kerstdag), 25 December 1784**. The inscription on the machine reads: *J. CUTHBERTSON Fecit Amsterdam MDCCLXXXIV*.

A collection of **first-generation carbon-filament incandescent electric lamps** displayed in a three-tiered mahogany rack (445 × 265 mm), acquired almost entirely at the **First International Exposition of Electricity, Paris, August–November 1881**, with a small number of Siemens lamps added in 1882. The ~20 bulbs represent the five principal competing manufacturers of the era: Thomas A. Edison, Joseph Wilson Swan, St. George Lane Fox-Pitt, Hiram Stevens Maxim, and Siemens & Halske (Berlin).

Lamps were purchased and subsequently tested by Teylers Museum's curator of the Physical Cabinet, **Elisa van der Ven** (1833–1909), who measured light output, temperature, and electrical resistance and published his findings in **1882** — the **first published scientific study of incandescent lamps in the Netherlands**. In 1883 Van der Ven used the lamps to propose electric lighting for the new Lecture Hall, a proposal rejected by museum leadership.

At the 1881 exposition, a testing committee (including William Crookes) ranked lamp efficiency: 1st Edison, 2nd Lane-Fox, 3rd Swan, 4th Maxim. The rack assembles exactly these five competing systems.

Despite its name, this is **not a weather instrument**. An "electric barometer" is a demonstration device for the phenomenon of **barometric light** — the mysterious glow produced when mercury moves inside an evacuated glass tube. First observed by **Jean Picard** in 1675, this phenomenon is now understood as **triboelectric gas discharge / electroluminescence**: mercury sliding against glass generates static charge, which excites residual gas molecules in the vacuum, causing them to emit light. It is the same fundamental mechanism as a **neon lamp** — making this instrument a direct ancestor of electric lighting, created 90 years before Edison. Made in 1790, it belongs to the research programme of **Martinus van Marum**, who operated the world's largest electrostatic generator at Teylers and had just established the first physics laboratory in the Netherlands that same year.
(not provided)
(not listed)

This is a **solar microscope** (*zonnemicroscoop*) — essentially the world's first projector — made by **J. Mortier** in Amsterdam in 1776. A solar microscope uses sunlight reflected through a condensing lens to project hugely magnified images of microscopic specimens onto a wall or screen in a darkened room. A flea could be projected to six feet wide. These instruments were simultaneously scientific tools and public entertainment devices, making microscopy a communal experience for the first time. The 1776 date places this instrument at the intersection of Pieter Teyler van der Hulst's lifetime collecting (he died 1778) and Martinus van Marum's early curatorship, making its provenance historically significant.
(not provided)

A set of **distillation vessels** (*destilleerketels*) made of **Wedgwood Queen's Ware** (cream-coloured earthenware / creamware), used in the chemistry laboratory of **Martinus van Marum** (1750–1837), founding director of Teylers Museum. Van Marum established the **first dedicated chemistry laboratory in the Netherlands** in 1790 in a building adjacent to the museum, using it to demonstrate and extend Lavoisier's oxygen-based chemistry to Dutch audiences.

Queen's Ware is the refined creamware body perfected by **Josiah Wedgwood I** (1730–1795) from c.1765, named after Queen Charlotte (who granted permission to use the name from 1767). Wedgwood was a well-documented supplier of laboratory apparatus — retorts, crucibles, evaporating dishes — to leading 18th-century chemists including Priestley and Lavoisier. These vessels were almost certainly acquired for van Marum's 1790 laboratory programme.

identified_by.content: Model van een ultracentrifuge voor het verrijken van uranium, naar Kistemaker

referred_to_by.content: This is a plastic scale model of an **ultracentrifuge for uranium enrichment**, made at the **FOM Laboratory for Mass Spectrography** (later AMOLF) in Amsterdam in 1957. It represents the foundational technology of the **Dutch ultracentrifuge program**, which led to the establishment of **URENCO** — one of the world's largest commercial uranium enrichment companies — via the Treaty of Almelo (1970) with the UK and Germany. The model was designed by **Jacob Kistemaker** (1917–2010), the physicist who founded the Dutch enrichment program and who was simultaneously **conservator (curator) of Teylers' Natuurkundig Kabinet** (Physics Cabinet). The technology descended from this program was infamously stolen by **A.Q. Khan** in 1972–1975 and proliferated to Pakistan, Iran, Libya, and North Korea.

This is a **Gregorian reflecting telescope** made by the Van der Bildt workshop in Franeker, Friesland — one of the most prolific telescope-making dynasties in 18th-century Europe. The patriarch **Jan Pieters van der Bildt** (1709–1791) manufactured over **550 reflecting telescopes** during his career, rivaling the output of James Short of London. According to a Teylers Museum article, this instrument is a remarkable **dual-system telescope** that can function in both Gregorian mode (concave secondary) and Cassegrain mode (convex secondary), with a brass tube of 630 mm (780 mm with extension), a primary mirror of 108 mm diameter in speculum metal, and Huygens eyepieces including a micrometer eyepiece. It is displayed in vitrine VIII of the Oval Room.
(not listed)

This is a Klankanalysator (Sound Analyzer), after Rudolph Koenig — the world's first acoustic spectrum analyzer. The instrument uses a bank of Helmholtz resonators connected to manometric flame capsules and a rotating mirror to make the harmonic components of complex sounds visible. When a sound (voice, musical instrument, etc.) is directed at the resonators, each one tuned to a different frequency vibrates in sympathy with its corresponding partial tone. These vibrations modulate gas flames via thin membranes, and the resulting flame fluctuations — invisible to the naked eye due to their speed — become visible as saw-toothed patterns of light when observed in a rotating four-sided mirror. The instrument is, in essence, the 19th-century equivalent of an oscilloscope or modern spectrum analyzer.
Total height 895 mm

A copper engraving by **Albrecht Dürer** (Nuremberg, 1471 – Nuremberg, 1528), dated 1504, depicting Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden. Bartsch 1, Meder 1 — the most prestigious numbering in both major Dürer catalogues. One of the foundational works of Northern Renaissance art, the engraving is the first known depiction of Adam and Eve that prioritized **ideal classical beauty** over the narrative of sin. Dürer modelled Adam on the Apollo Belvedere and Eve on the Medici Venus, deploying contrapposto to demonstrate perfect human proportions. More preparatory drawings survive for this print than for any other Dürer engraving. The four animals in the scene — cat, rabbit, ox, and elk — represent the four **humoral temperaments** in pre-lapsarian harmony.

referred_to_by.content: Oil painting on canvas (52.1 × 41.4 cm, signed and dated 1825) by Dutch painter and actor **Johannes Rienksz. Jelgerhuis** (1770–1836). The painting depicts the **choir of the Nieuwe Kerk (New Church) in Delft**, looking toward the monumental mausoleum of **Willem I, Prince of Orange** (William the Silent, 1533–1584), designed by **Hendrick de Keyser** (1614–1621) and completed by his son **Pieter de Keyser** in 1623. The painting belongs to the tradition of Dutch topographical church-interior painting stretching from the 17th-century Delft School (Houckgeest, De Witte, Van Vliet) to 19th-century Romanticism. Staffage figures include a man and young boy with a dog. The canvas was painted in the year Jelgerhuis exhibited in Haarlem. The Rijksmuseum holds a related chalk/watercolor drawing dated 1824 (RP-T-00-1820) that appears to be a direct preparatory study for this painting, and a second related work from July 1819 (RP-T-1921-334).

referred_to_by.content: Oil painting on canvas (98.1 × 120.1 cm, signed and dated 1838) by Dutch painter **Nicolaas Pieneman** (1809–1860). The painting depicts the immediate aftermath of the assassination attempt on William I, Prince of Orange ("William the Silent") by **Juan de Jáuregui** in Antwerp on 18 March 1582. The scene shows the wounded Willem I in a chair, his wife Charlotte of Bourbon attending him, the dead body of Jáuregui on the floor, and the young **Prince Maurits** (age 14–15) directing soldiers to examine the assassin's corpse. A 19th-century Dutch nationalist history painting acquired by Teylers in or around 1838 — the year the museum's First Painting Gallery opened. A steel engraving after this painting was made by Johann Wilhelm Kaiser (I) in 1853–54 for the *Vereeniging ter Bevordering van Beeldende Kunsten* (Teylers KG 08154).

A large oil painting on canvas by **Isaac Israëls** (Amsterdam, 1865 – The Hague, 1934), depicting a North African woman in loose-fitting attire beating a drum before a Parisian fairground wrestling tent. Painted during Israëls' Paris period (c. 1904–1913), the painting shows a drummer performing the role of street "barker" to attract spectators to a sideshow. A poster of wrestlers is visible on the wall behind her. The Teylers version (KS 165) is one of at least four known versions of this composition by Israëls; the Rijksmuseum holds a closely related work (SK-C-983). An oil painting on panel (63 x 81 cm) by **Wybrand Hendriks** (Amsterdam, 1744 – Haarlem, 1831), dated 1799, depicting a session of the **Haarlemse Teekencollegie** — the private drawing society that Hendriks himself founded in 1796 as successor to the dissolved *Haarlemse Stads Teekenacademie* (1772–1795). Members are shown drawing from plaster casts of classical antiquities (Venus de Medici, Venus Callipyge, Apollo Belvedere, and a Vulcan's forge group). Hendriks appears at far right. Six figures are named in the catalog: van Brussel, Visser Bender, Claterbos, Milatz, van der Willigen, Gerlings. Donated to Teylers in 1987 by Mrs. J.H.M. Staring-de Mol van Otterloo. Exhibited in the major 2023 Teylers retrospective *"Wybrand Hendriks was hier!"*. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.

A small portrait drawing on white paper (175 × 126 mm), in black medium, depicting **August Friedrich Schmidt** (11 February 1795, Anklam, Pomerania – 12 September 1899, Wolgast, Pomerania) — celebrated at his death as the **last surviving German veteran of the Napoleonic Wars of Liberation (1813–1815)** and, by many accounts, **the last surviving combatant of the Battle of Waterloo** of any nationality. Schmidt enlisted as a volunteer Jäger in the 1st Pomeranian Infantry Regiment on 17 March 1813 and fought at Bautzen, Großbeeren, Dennewitz, Leipzig, Laon, Ligny, and Waterloo. He worked as a goldsmith in Wolgast and died seven months after his 104th birthday.

The drawing shows Schmidt in bust format, wearing three medals and a soft house cap, with the inscription *"August Schmidt / der letzte Freiheitskämpfer von 1813–15"* below. Accompanying the drawing are two sheets of handwritten German text in Kurrent script sourced from **Über Land und Meer** (Stuttgart illustrated weekly), No. 52, 1898–1899, with the portrait published with permission of the Deutschen Kriegerbund-Buchhandlung, Tempelhof-Berlin. The object was acquired in 1989 from the collection formerly belonging to **Adriaan van der Willigen Pz.** (1810–1876), Haarlem physician, art historian, and member of Teylers Tweede Genootschap.

referred_to_by.content: A deeply personal mixed-media drawing by **Marlene Dumas** (b. 1953, Cape Town), made in 1989 during the final days of her pregnancy with her daughter **Helena** (born 11 April 1989). Dumas is widely regarded as **one of the most important living figurative painters**. In May 2025, her painting *Miss January* sold at Christie's for **$13.63 million** — the record for the most expensive work by a living woman artist at auction. In November 2025, she became the **first contemporary woman artist to enter the Louvre's permanent collection**.

referred_to_by.content:
M 00605 is a substantial malachite specimen (409.6 g) from the Ural Mountains of Russia, collected during the 18th century and now displayed in the Oval Hall (Ovale Zaal) of Teylers Museum. Malachite is a copper carbonate hydroxide mineral with the formula Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂, crystallising in the monoclinic crystal system, with a hardness of 3.5–4 on the Mohs scale. It is characterised by its vivid green colour with distinctive concentric banding patterns formed by the alternating growth of botryoidal or stalactitic masses.

The catalog image shows a spectacular specimen displaying the mineral's characteristic vivid green colour with concentric banding in lighter and darker green, visible on a polished cross-section that occupies most of the specimen's upper surface. The base is brownish-red, likely a gossan or iron oxide host rock typical of Ural copper deposits. The specimen is visually striking and representative of the finest-quality malachite extracted from the Ural Mountains during the 18th century.

The specimen has a documented and distinguished provenance. It was originally part of the mineral collection of the Dutch naturalist **Johannes le Francq van Berkhey** (1729–1812), a physician, painter, poet, and natural historian best known for his *Natuurlyke Historie van Holland* (4 vols., 1769–1776). Van Berkhey's collection was illustrated by **Martinus Houttuyn** (1720–1798), who included an engraving of this very malachite specimen in his encyclopedic work *Natuurlyke Historie of uitvoerige Beschryving der Dieren, Planten en Mineraalen, volgens het Samenstel van den Heer Linnaeus* (37 volumes, 1761–1785). When van Berkhey's collection was forced onto the market following his dismissal from Leiden University in 1795, Teylers Museum director **Martinus van Marum** (1750–1837) acquired the specimen for the museum. It has remained in the Teylers collection ever since and is displayed in the Oval Hall.
Weight: 409.6 g

M 01518 is a large cluster of quartz crystals (rock crystal, i.e. colourless transparent quartz) from the Dauphiné region of the French Alps, south of Grenoble. The specimen weighs 6,900 grams and was acquired around 1802. The photograph shows a dense, multi-directional cluster of elongated prismatic crystals with pyramidal terminations, predominantly colourless to pale cream-yellow in tone, with some brownish iron-staining at the crystal bases. The crystals display the characteristic hexagonal prism faces and rhombohedral pyramid faces typical of alpha-quartz. No matrix rock is visible; the cluster appears to be a free-standing druse.

The Dauphiné region (now part of Isère, Drôme and Hautes-Alpes departments) has been one of the world's premier localities for rock crystal since at least the 17th century. The Oisans massif, particularly the La Gardette mine near Bourg-d'Oisans, produced some of the finest quartz specimens in European collections. Alpine crystal hunters ("cristalliers") worked these deposits commercially, supplying Parisian, Genevan and Milanese gem cutters as well as collectors. The term "cristal de roche" — literally "rock crystal" — was the standard French name for colourless quartz in 18th-century natural history literature and trade.

The specimen dates to the period when Teylers director Martinus van Marum was actively building the mineral cabinet. The production date of "circa 1802" in the catalog most likely refers to the date of acquisition by Teylers rather than any date of formation or collection in the field. Quartz is of course a geological mineral millions of years in the making, making the phrase "production date" categorically inappropriate for a natural specimen.

M 01524 is a large, visually spectacular specimen of stibnite (antimony trisulfide, Sb₂S₃) from the Ichinokawa Mine, Saijō, Ehime Prefecture, Japan — one of the most celebrated mineral localities in the world. The catalog image shows a towering cluster of bladed prismatic crystals displaying the characteristic lead-grey to black metallic lustre and bright mirror-like cleavage faces of stibnite. The crystals are elongated parallel to the c-axis with characteristic striated faces and grow in a complex fan-like or sheaf arrangement, reaching impressive height. The base consists of a mass of smaller interlocking crystals. There is no obvious matrix rock — the specimen appears to consist almost entirely of stibnite crystals, which is typical of Ichinokawa material.

The Ichinokawa Mine is located on Shikoku Island, near the city of Saijō (formerly Saijō-chō) in Ehime Prefecture, Japan. It operated as an antimony mine from at least the 7th century (tradition holds it supplied antimony for the Great Buddha of Nara, cast in 748 CE) and reached peak production between 1875 and 1900, when it produced 16,600 tons of antimony. The mine closed around 1957–1962. During the late 19th century, Ichinokawa produced the largest and finest stibnite crystals ever documented: single crystals up to approximately 60 cm long and 15 cm in diameter. Specimens were exhibited at the Kyoto Exhibition (1871) and the Paris World's Fair (1878), where they won silver medals. For over a century, Ichinokawa was considered the premier world locality for stibnite.

M 03353 is one of the most historically significant geological specimens in any museum collection in the world: a fragment of rock chipped from the highest ice-free exposed rock outcrop at the summit of Mont Blanc on 3 August 1787, by the Genevan scientist Horace-Bénédict de Saussure (1740–1799) during his ascent. The rock outcrop from which it was taken, the Rocher de la Tournette, stands at approximately 4,677 metres on the summit ridge of Mont Blanc. The specimen weighs 147.7 grams and shows an irregular shape consistent with having been chipped from an exposed rock face.

The specimen is a **biotite granite** — specifically a leucocratic monzogranite — the dominant rock type forming the Mont Blanc massif. The granite consists of quartz, potassium feldspar (sometimes large white rectangular crystals), plagioclase, and black biotite mica, with the biotite partially replaced by greenish chlorite as a metamorphic alteration product. Both catalog images confirm this: one shows the fresh broken surface with a granitic speckled texture in grey-pink tones; the other shows the rougher, slightly more weathered outer surface with visible greenish chlorite patches and a museum label reading "W47 / M3353."

De Saussure's ascent (3 August 1787) was the third overall ascent of Mont Blanc and the second by a scientist. It was a major scientific expedition: he brought 18 guides, scientific instruments including barometers, thermometers, a hygrometer, a magnetometer, a cyanometer (to measure sky blueness), and an electrometer. At the summit he made 17 hours of observations, measured barometric pressure to calculate summit altitude (arriving at 4,775 m — within 30 m of the modern value of 4,805.59 m), and collected rock samples. His findings were published in his multi-volume *Voyages dans les Alpes* (1779–1796).

The specimen was acquired by Teylers Museum in 1802, purchased by director Martinus van Marum from de Saussure's son Nicolas-Théodore de Saussure, three years after Horace-Bénédict's death. It is displayed in the Oval Room alongside the Exchaquet maquette (M 12369).

M 11800 is a collection of wooden crystal models made in pear wood, commissioned by or under the direction of French abbé René Just Haüy (28 February 1743 – 1 June 1822) to serve as three-dimensional teaching aids accompanying his landmark four-volume publication *Traité de Minéralogie* (1801). Haüy, professor of mineralogy at the Muséum national d'Histoire naturelle in Paris and widely regarded as the founder of modern crystallography, devised the mathematical theory that crystal forms arise from the stacking of fundamental "integrant molecules" — a theory he called the law of rational intercepts (later formalised as the law of rational indices). His models demonstrated these crystal forms in tangible, measurable three-dimensional objects, with each model precisely crafted with accurate dihedral angles.

The models were hand-carved in pear wood by a woodworking family and made available for purchase alongside the *Traité*. Teylers Museum director Martinus van Marum corresponded with Haüy and purchased 597 models between 1802 and 1804, paying for them through correspondence. Van Marum used the models to reorganise the museum's mineral collection according to Haüy's crystallographic system, displaying some in the central showcase of the Oval Room and storing the remainder in five large drawers. The models range from approximately 2.5 to 10 cm in size. Each model bears a label in iron-gall ink, reportedly written by Haüy himself, giving the crystal form name.

According to the authoritative study by Wim Saeijs (*Mineralogical Record*, vol. 39 no. 5, 2008), **565 of the original 597 models survive at Teylers**, making this the most complete collection of Haüy crystal models in the world. The Teylers catalog and topstukken page, however, state the number as **550** — an undercounting of 15 specimens.

No image is currently available in the online catalog for M 11800 (the image request returns a "not found" placeholder).

M 12369 is a large, three-dimensional terrain relief model of the Mont Blanc massif, created in 1787 by the Swiss engineer and cartographer Charles-François Exchaquet (6 December 1746 – 1792). The model depicts the Mont Blanc summit and the surrounding Chamonix valley in painted relief at a scale of approximately 1:15,000, measuring approximately 100 × 64 × 35 cm. It is constructed from nine glued wooden blocks carved in Swiss stone pine (arolla pine), shaped with chisels to the correct topography, finished with plaster, and covered with ground quartz to represent the glacial snowfields. It was painted, with snow cover reflecting August conditions (the prime climbing season).

The catalog image shows the front face of the model as seen from the Chamonix valley side. The high central peak (Mont Blanc summit) is visible at upper right with white-painted glaciers and snow, while the valley and rock faces are rendered in brown-ochre tones. The green-painted base is visible at the lower edge. The model is extraordinarily detailed for its period, made by a man who had personally explored the massif. Exchaquet served as Directeur général des Mines et Fonderies du Haut-Faucigny from his base in Servoz, near Chamonix, from 1780 until his death in 1792.

Exchaquet made or commissioned multiple copies of this maquette; as of recent scholarship, five copies were known to have been made, of which two definitive survivals are documented: one at Teylers Museum and one at the Muséum d'histoire naturelle de la Ville de Genève. The Teylers copy was acquired in 1799 — either purchased by director Martinus van Marum for nine guilders, or donated by Amsterdam merchant Govert Lups (sources differ on this point). It is displayed in the Oval Room of Teylers Museum alongside the De Saussure rock specimen (M 03353), with a dotted line tracing the 1787 ascent route.

This is a red chalk drawing by Hendrick Goltzius (1558–1617), depicting the famous equestrian statue of Marcus Aurelius on the Capitoline Hill in Rome. It was made during Goltzius' transformative Italian journey of 1590–1591. The drawing is one of approximately 125 Goltzius sheets held by Teylers Museum — the largest collection of Goltzius drawings in the world. It was acquired in 1790 from the Odescalchi heirs in Rome, as part of a purchase of approximately 1,700 drawings that had passed through the collection of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Marcus Aurelius (emperor), horseman, statue, sculpture, Rome: Campidoglio
Teylers Museum, aangekocht van de Odescalchi erfgenamen te Rome, 1790

A small **silverpoint** (*metaalstift*) portrait drawing on prepared parchment (vellum), 45 × 33 mm, by **Hendrick Goltzius** (1558–1617). It depicts **Jacques de la Faille** (c.1548–1615), a wealthy Antwerp merchant and Colonel of the Antwerp Civic Guard, and was made as a **preparatory study** (*tafelette*) for the engraved portrait New Hollstein 216, published in Haarlem by Harmen Adolfsz. in 1580, on the occasion of de la Faille's marriage to Josina Hamels. The inscription on the drawing itself reads *"voor de prent"* (for the print), explicitly confirming its preparatory function. De la Faille later fled Spanish-controlled Antwerp for Haarlem in 1584. The finished engraving is held in the Rijksmuseum, Philadelphia, Princeton, the Art Institute of Chicago, and Musea Brugge. The Teylers drawing belongs to a group of nearly one hundred surviving Goltzius *silverpoint tafeletten* — the largest such surviving group anywhere in the world.

A pen-and-ink drawing (*penwerk*) by **Hendrick Goltzius** (Mühlbracht, 1558 – Haarlem, 1617), dated 1588, depicting his own permanently crippled right hand. As an infant of approximately one year, Goltzius fell into a pan of boiling oil; a meddling neighbour removed the splints and bound only the right hand in cloth, causing the fingers to permanently contract into a claw-like configuration. The drawing belongs to Goltzius's celebrated category of "pen works" (*penwerken*) — virtuoso pen-and-ink drawings in which he deliberately reproduced, using a pen, the swelling and tapering burin lines of his engraving technique. The hand is simultaneously a study, a technical demonstration, a trademark, and an indirect **self-portrait**. During his incognito Italian journey (1590–91), the drawing reportedly served as proof of his identity when other artists recognised the famous deformed hand. One of the most celebrated drawings in the entire Teylers collection. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.
*(none)*

referred_to_by.content: A large drawing on paper (357 x 478 mm) by **Rembrandt Harmenszoon van Rijn** (Leiden, 1606 – Amsterdam, 1669), inscribed and dated **"Rembrandt f 1634"**, depicting ten disciples arranged in a circle around Christ in the **Garden of Gethsemane** on the night before his arrest (Matthew 26:36–46; Mark 14:32–42; Luke 22:39–46). Christ's illuminated head and gesture suggest he is speaking; two disciples sleep, one yawns prominently. A strip of paper was removed from the right edge "in antiquity," accounting for the absence of the eleventh or twelfth disciple. Executed in pen in brown and black ink, brush in grey and brown wash, black, red and green chalk, and white heightening. Catalogued as **Benesch 89** in the standard catalogue raisonné. One of Rembrandt's largest and most technically elaborate drawings. Acquired by Teylers at auction in **1796 for 60 guilders**. Exhibited at the Louvre's major exhibition *Rembrandt et la figure du Christ* (2011). Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.

A collaborative New Year's greeting collage on paper (330 x 412 mm) made in late **1970** by **Niki de Saint-Phalle** (Neuilly-sur-Seine, 1930 – La Jolla, 2002) and **Jean Tinguely** (Fribourg, 1925 – Bern, 1991), sent for New Year **1971** to **Edy de Wilde** (then director of the Stedelijk Museum Amsterdam) and his partner **Nono Reinhold**. Executed in collage, felt-tip marker, ballpoint pen, and adhesive tape. The inscription thanks de Wilde for dinner and invites the recipients to visit the couple at **Soisy-sur-Ecole** — their studio/residence 44 km southeast of Paris, also the site of their ongoing work on the monumental sculpture **Le Cyclop** (begun 1969). A gouache on paper (272 x 360 mm) by **Cornelis Troost** (Amsterdam, 1696 – Amsterdam, 1750), dated **1742**, depicting a crowd assembled before the inn **'t Bokje** in the **Haarlemmerhout** to witness an elaborate prank orchestrated by writer-satirist **Jacob Campo Weyerman** (1677–1747) and associates in 1739: a figure exposes painted buttocks arranged to mimic a face in a first-floor window, with blackened trumpeters and mock-ceremonial fanfare, parodying public fascination with the Moroccan ambassador. This is one of at least four treatments Troost made of this subject. Acquired by Teylers in **1780 for 100 guilders** — a considerable sum for a drawing at the time. Designated one of Teylers' **50 Topstukken**.

"Drie tulpen" (Three Tulips) is a large-format watercolor drawing by Maria Sibylla Merian (1647–1717) on parchment, dated ca. 1670–1675. It depicts three tulips rendered with exceptional botanical precision, executed in pencil, colored brush, black brush, grey brush, and black pen. The sheet bears multiple handwritten inscriptions including the artist's own signature variants. It belongs to a category of luxury commercial drawings on parchment that Merian produced for the art market during her Nuremberg years (1670–1685). The work is cataloged as a "Topstuk" (masterpiece) by the museum.

referred_to_by.content: This is a cast silver medal depicting Jan Hus (c. 1369/1370–1415), the Czech religious reformer who was burned at the stake as a heretic at the Council of Constance on 6 July 1415. The obverse shows Hus's portrait bust; the reverse depicts his execution with Latin inscriptions including his famous prophecy. The medal belongs to the well-documented "Hustaler" tradition of Reformation commemorative medals. Despite the "1415" date inscribed on the medal, it was **not manufactured in 1415** — it is a later commemorative piece, most likely from the early 18th century (c. 1700–1740). It was part of the personal collection of Pieter Teyler van der Hulst, the museum's founder.

referred_to_by.content: This is a rare **gold** example of a "Judenmedaille" (Prague Jewish medal) depicting Empress Eleanor of Portugal (1434–1467), wife of Emperor Frederick III. It belongs to a well-documented series of pseudo-medieval cast medals produced in Prague c. 1600–1650 to glorify the Habsburg dynasty's ancestry. The medal features Eleanor enthroned in full imperial regalia on the obverse, with a rose design on the reverse accompanied by Latin verses praising her virtue. Gold examples of this series are described as "particularly rare" in the numismatic literature; no other gold example of the Eleanor subject has been identified at auction.

referred_to_by.content: This is a **prinsendaalder** — a large silver coin struck in 1584 at the Dordrecht mint, bearing the portrait of **William the Silent (Willem van Oranje)** with a sword over his shoulder. It is the **only official Dutch coin to depict William of Orange**, and the 1584 date places it at the exact historical fulcrum of his assassination on **10 July 1584** by Balthasar Gerard. The coin was designed as political propaganda by the States of Holland, intended to facilitate William's formal inauguration as sovereign — which never took place. Dies were engraved by **Gerard van Bylaer**, one of the most important medallists of the Dutch Revolt, and the coin was produced under mint master **Jacob Janszoon de Jonge** at the Dordrecht mint, which had operated continuously since 1367.
Obverse: helmeted coat of arms of Holland. Reverse: bust right, Willem van Oranje with sword over shoulder

referred_to_by.content: This is a silver triumph medal commemorating one of the most celebrated military exploits in Dutch history — the **Capture of Breda on 4 March 1590** using the "Turfschip van Breda" (Peat Ship of Breda), often compared to the **Trojan Horse**. Approximately 68–75 Dutch and English soldiers under **Charles de Héraugière** hid inside a peat barge, sailed unchecked past Spanish guards into the castle, and captured it at dawn. The medal was struck by order of the States-General just **16 days** after the event (resolution of 20 March 1590), with dies engraved by **Gerard van Bylaer** at the Dordrecht mint. It comes from the personal collection of **Pieter Teyler van der Hulst** — the museum's founder — demonstrating his active interest in Dutch historical medals.

carried_out_by._label: Christoffel Adolphi (Adolfszoon) (±1631-1684) — medailleur; Jan Zoet (1609-1674) — designer; Republiek der Zeven Verenigde Nederlanden, Holland (1581-1795) — issuing authority

referred_to_by.content:
TMNK 00741 is a large silver commemorative medal struck in Amsterdam in 1667 to celebrate the Peace of Breda, which ended the Second Anglo-Dutch War (1665–1667). The treaty was signed on 31 July 1667 in the city of Breda, Netherlands. The medal was struck by order of the States of Holland (Staten van Holland) and is one of the most artistically ambitious Dutch medals of the seventeenth century.

The medal is the work of Christoffel Adolphi (also known as Adolfszoon, c. 1631–1684), the leading Dutch medal engraver of the period, working in Amsterdam. The inscriptions were composed by the Amsterdam poet Jan Zoet (1609–1674), a prolific writer known for his satirical and politically engaged verse. The obverse celebrates Dutch triumph in the war — most vividly symbolized by the Raid on the Medway (June 1667), during which Admiral Michiel de Ruyter's fleet entered the Thames estuary and destroyed the English fleet at anchor. The reverse celebrates the peace itself and the resumption of trade.

The medal is notable for a politically explosive element: the figure being trampled by Hollandia on the obverse was identified by contemporaries as **Discord** (Tweedracht), but the motto "PROCUL . HINC . MALA . BESTIA . REGNIS" ("Be gone, pernicious beast, from our realms") was widely understood as an insult directed personally at King Charles II of England. The British government formally protested; the Dutch States apologized and ordered the dies destroyed. That TMNK 00741 survives is evidence that some strikes were made before the dies were suppressed — giving this medal historical significance beyond its commemorative function. The medal is referenced in the standard corpus of Dutch historical medals compiled by Gerard van Loon.
* NUMISMA * POSTERITATI * SACRUM * BELGA * BRITANNOQUE * RECONCILIATIS . / C . A .
Acquired 1778-04-08 from Pieter Teyler van der Hulst

_label: Rampzalige reis van Lourens Thyszoon Veyselaer en Daniël Silleman met het schip De Gouden Buys

identified_by.content: Rampzalige reis van Lourens Thyszoon Veyselaer en Daniël Silleman met het schip De Gouden Buys

carried_out_by._label: Dirk Schelte (1639–1715) — maker; Unknown medalist; Dutch Republic — Holland Province (1581–1795) — issuing authority

referred_to_by.content:
TMNK 01297 is a large silver engraved medal commemorating one of the most dramatic survival stories in VOC history: the disaster of the ship De Gouden Buys (The Golden Pipe) in 1693. The medal was made in 1695, two years after the event, when one of the two survivors — Lourens Thyszoon Veyselaer — had already died on his return voyage. The other survivor, Daniël Silleman, had published an account of the ordeal in Enkhuizen in 1695, which appears to have directly prompted the medal's commission.

The Gouden Buys departed Enkhuizen on 4 May 1693, bound for the Dutch East Indies (the VOC's trading empire in Asia). During the passage through the tropics, the ship was becalmed for two months near the equator. The combination of heat, thirst, and disease — most likely scurvy and dysentery — killed the great majority of the crew. When the ship finally reached the coast of southern Africa and anchored in Helenabay (St. Helena Bay, approximately 100 km north of what is now Cape Town) on 19 October 1693, only about 12 men remained fit for duty. Of the crew of approximately 190, around 178 had died at sea. Captain Baanman was bedridden. Seven men went ashore in a longboat to seek help. Five of them died in the African wilderness. Two survived: Lourens Thyszoon Veyselaer, who was taken in by the Khoikhoi people and eventually led to the Dutch settlement at the Cape; and Daniël Silleman.

Governor Simon van der Stel organized a rescue mission with four ships. The salvage vessel *Dageraad* recovered treasure chests from the stricken Gouden Buys (which had dragged her anchors and grounded in shallow water) and took Silleman aboard. But the *Dageraad* then foundered in a storm off Robben Island, with the loss of over 200 more lives. Silleman survived by swimming ashore. Both Veyselaer and Silleman ultimately made their way back to the Cape together. Veyselaer died on the homeward voyage. Silleman returned to the Netherlands, where his account was published and the medal was struck in his honor.

The medal is engraved rather than struck — its technique (smooth with file marks and engraved lines) makes it a unique pictorial object rather than a mass-produced coin-type piece. The inscriptions were composed by the Amsterdam jeweler-poet Dirk Schelte (baptized Amsterdam 4 October 1639, buried Amsterdam 26 March 1715). The commissioning body was the VOC Chamber of Enkhuizen — the same chamber from whose port the Gouden Buys had departed. The medal was awarded to Veyselaer (and/or Silleman) as an honor, with a suspension ring for wearing on a chain.

TMNK 02269 is a large gold medal — one of the most imposing in the Teylers numismatic collection — awarded posthumously by the Staten-Generaal (States-General of the Dutch Republic) to the family of Wolter Jan Gerrit baron Bentinck (1745–1781), in recognition of his conduct at the Battle of the Doggersbank (5 August 1781). Bentinck commanded the 54-gun ship *Batavier* in the Dutch squadron under Vice-Admiral Johan Arnold Zoutman during this engagement, which took place in the North Sea during the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War (1780–1784). He died of his wounds on 24 August 1781, nineteen days after the battle.

The Battle of the Doggersbank was fought between a Dutch convoy escort and a British squadron under Vice-Admiral Sir Hyde Parker. Both sides suffered heavy casualties; neither achieved a decisive tactical victory, but the Dutch merchant convoy successfully reached port. In the Netherlands, the battle was celebrated as a vindication of Dutch naval honour after decades of decline. Zoutman became a national hero. The States-General awarded a small number of large gold honor medals on gold chains — known as the "Erepenning van de Staten-Generaal" — to the most senior officers: Zoutman, several captains-at-sea (Dedel, Van Braam, Van Kinsbergen), and posthumously Bentinck.

This medal is distinct from the oval "Doggersbank-medaille" (Betts-588; Van Loon Supplement 563) designed by Franciscus Hemsterhuis and engraved by Johann Heinrich Schepp, which was awarded more widely to all officers and petty officers present at the battle. The Teylers piece is the rarer, heavier States-General type — a prestige award on a gold chain.

The gold medal is screwed (it unscrews, presumably to allow it to be hung on a chain or worn as a decorative object). At 90.58 mm diameter and 811 g, it is an exceptionally large and heavy gold object — one of the largest and heaviest single medals in Dutch eighteenth-century medallurgy.
CONCORDIA · RES PARVAE · CRESCUNT
DISCORDIA MAXIMAE DILABUNTUR; HONI · SOIT · QVI · MAL · Y · PENSE; IE MAINTIENDRAI
TER BELOONINIG VAN DE DAPPERHEIT, GETOONT IN DE ACTIE OP DOGGERSBANK . 5 AUG 1781

This is an extraordinarily rare **gold striking** of a coin normally produced in silver — a quarter guilder (5 stuivers) of the Province of Holland, dated 1692. Gold strikings (*gouden afslagen*) are off-metal presentation pieces produced in tiny numbers (sometimes just one or two) for dignitaries or mint officials, using the standard silver-coin dies. They are among the rarest and most valuable items in Dutch numismatics; comparable gold strikings have sold at auction for **€180,000–€245,000**. The dies were engraved by **Daniël Drappentier** (1643–1714), Engraver General of Holland, West Friesland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, and the coin was produced at the Dordrecht mint under mint master **Mattheus Sonnemans**. It was bequeathed to Teylers in 1896 by **Adriaan Justus Enschedé** — owner of the famous Joh. Enschedé printing firm, printers of Dutch banknotes since 1814.
MO : NO . ARGENT : COMIT : HOLL : 1692 / 5 S; HAC NITIMVR HANC TVEMVR
Legaat Adriaan Justus Enschedé, 1896

This is a portrait etching by Karel Dujardin (c. 1626–1678), depicting the Amsterdam poet and glazier Jan Vos (1612–1667) half-length, facing slightly right, holding a rolled paper or parchment in his left hand. The print was created as the frontispiece portrait for the second volume of Vos's collected poems, *Alle de gedichten* (Amsterdam, 1662). Below the portrait is a four-line laudatory poem by Joost van den Vondel. The print exists in at least 3–4 states; later states include biographical text about Vos's role in the 1648 Peace of Munster celebrations.
De Hollandse dichter Jan Vos met in zijn linkerhand een rol papier (of perkament).

A large-format drawing on blue-grey paper (497 × 349 mm) attributed to **Peter Paul Rubens** (1577–1640), made as a copy after **Tiziano Vecellio (Titian)** (c.1488–1576). Subject: **Saint Jerome in the Wilderness** (*Heilige Hieronymus in de woestijn*). The drawing is in mixed media: black chalk, white and light yellow heightening, red chalk accents, brown pen (worked up), with a brown pen border line. The inscription on the sheet reads *"PP Rubbens St Hironimus"* — a later collector's attribution annotation, not an autograph signature. Additional inscribed numbers (113, C T 98, 899) are old inventory/sale marks from previous collections. Titian painted Saint Jerome in at least four distinct versions (c.1531, Louvre; c.1552, Brera; c.1575, Escorial; Thyssen-Bornemisza). Rubens studied Titian's works intensively during his Italian sojourn (1600–1608) and at the Spanish court in Madrid (1628–29), where he had direct access to Titian's Saint Jerome at the Escorial.

The catalog bibliography includes two Van Dyck titles specifically concerning Van Dyck's Saint Jerome paintings — indicating a live attribution debate that the record itself entirely conceals.

This is the double elephant folio first edition of *The Birds of America* by John James Audubon (1785–1851), published in London by the author between 1827 and 1838. It consists of 435 hand-coloured plates made using etching, engraving, aquatint, and drypoint on "double elephant" format paper (approximately 99 × 66 cm per sheet), depicting 489 species of North American birds in life size. The plates are numbered I–CCCCXXXV (Roman numerals); the first ten were engraved by William Home Lizars of Edinburgh and later retouched and aquatinted by Robert Havell Jr.; plates 11–435 were engraved and printed by Robert Havell Sr. and Robert Havell Jr. of London. The work was issued serially in 87 parts of five plates each to subscribers, and was subsequently bound by recipients — most commonly into four volumes. Teylers Museum acquired its copy directly by subscription during the years of publication (1827–1838), paying a total of 2,243 guilders — more than the annual salary of then-director Martinus van Marum. Teylers' copy is the only original double elephant folio edition of *The Birds of America* in the Netherlands, and one of approximately 119–120 complete copies known to survive worldwide.

---

One of the most celebrated botanical publications of the nineteenth century. *Jardin de la Malmaison* documents the exotic plant collection assembled by **Empress Joséphine de Beauharnais** at Château de Malmaison, west of Paris. Text by **Étienne Pierre Ventenat** (1757–1808), French botanist and former director of the Sainte-Geneviève library, engaged directly by Joséphine. Illustrated by **Pierre-Joseph Redouté** (1759–1840), the pre-eminent botanical illustrator of the era, later appointed official flower painter to the Empress.

Published in Paris by Imprimerie de Crapelet, issued in **20 fascicles of 6 plates each**, April 1803 – November 1805. Title pages carry Republican calendar dates An XI–XII (1803–1804), but the complete work extended to November 1805. Large folio (c.520 × 338 mm), two volumes bound as one. Contains **120 color-printed stipple-engraved plates** after Redouté watercolors, executed *à la poupée* (colors applied directly to the copperplate) and finished by hand by ~15 engravers. Only approximately **200 copies** were printed, most distributed as imperial presentation gifts to European monarchs and dignitaries. Ventenat described species new to science; the Malmaison gardens contained ~200 plant species flowering in France for the first time, including 46 Australian species.
